1:
The witnesses open the chapter's tragedy by setting the stage, and the grammatical-historical commentators do the surveying. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott agree that this Philistine muster was no ordinary border raid but, as Ellicott notes from Josephus, a supreme national effort: the enemy now stands in the very heart of the land, marching up the coast and turning inland to the broad plain of Jezreel, or Esdraelon. Clarke adds the circumstances that emboldened them — Sir Isaac Newton's conjecture that their ranks were swollen by men whom Amasis had driven out of Egypt, together with Samuel's death and David's disgrace, from all of which the Philistines had everything to hope.

The same school reads David's plight with unsparing honesty. Ellicott judges that David was now reaping the fruit of his own error: want of faith and patience had driven him to take refuge with the hereditary foes of his people, and the sixteen months in Philistia had been a life of duplicity and bloodshed, so that Achish's summons to his military vassal was 'perfectly natural' — the Philistine lords even dreamed of setting David on Saul's throne as their puppet. Ellicott traces the long shadow of that error still further, to the crushing national defeat David narrowly escaped sharing, the seven years' delay of his own recognition as king, and the civil war and eventual division of the tribes whose seeds were sown in these very months. Keil, however, keeps the covenant in view: David had fled thither only in extremity, and what more he had done was only to save his own life, so that the faithful covenant God would very soon rescue him and end Saul's persecution by Saul's own death in this war.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the moral. Had Israel not forsaken God there would have been no Philistines left to molest them, and had Saul not forsaken him they would long since have been put out of all danger. As for David's answer to Achish — surely thou shalt know what thy servant can do — Henry reads it as a studied ambiguity by which David keeps himself free from any promise to fight against Israel and yet keeps up Achish's expectation of help, so that the king, taking it for an engagement, promised in return to make him captain of his guards for life.
2:
The whole company fastens on the calculated vagueness of David's reply, and the debate is simply what he meant by it. The reformed Gill lays out the possibilities: the words might mean what David could do for the Philistines against Israel, or (as he secretly intended) what he could do for Israel against the Philistines; but Gill's own preference is that David was quite undetermined and at a loss, hoping that God in his providence would extricate him from the dilemma, as indeed he did.

The wesleyan-arminian voices take the reply as deliberately equivocal. Clarke calls it 'another equivocal answer' that could only be understood by David's later conduct — it might favour either side — while Achish understood it in the former sense and therefore promised, I will make thee keeper of my head for ever, that is, captain of my life-guards. Wesley compresses it: 'he speaks ambiguously, as he did before.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott feels the moral tension most keenly. Sorely perplexed, David temporises; he dares not refuse, yet the thought of commanding a Philistine division in a war against his own people is a terrible alternative. Achish, like so many whom the winning son of Jesse met, was completely won by David's generous character and would not see any ambiguity in the answer, at once offering him the command of the royal body-guard, an appointment which Ewald understood the king's words to signify. But Ellicott is careful to add that the king's blind confidence was not shared by the Philistine chieftains, whose jealousy of the distinguished stranger would soon open their eyes to David's real feelings (1 Sam. 29:3, 29:11); whispers of David's raids during the past year, he adds, were probably already current in some Philistine quarters. The puritan-evangelical Henry, from his comment on the previous verse, catches the diplomatic cunning: David keeps himself free from any promise to serve and yet keeps up the expectation of it, so that Achish took the words for an engagement and rewarded them accordingly. Beneath the differing shades, the witnesses agree on the essential thing: David's words are a piece of dangerous fencing, and it is Providence, not his own cleverness, that will finally deliver him from a service he could not in conscience perform.
3:
This verse is the narrator's deliberate parenthesis, and the witnesses read it as two hinges on which the coming story turns. Gill and Ellicott agree that the notice of Samuel's death — already recorded at 1 Sam. 25:1 — is repeated here for a reason: partly to explain why the Philistines grew bold and Saul afraid, and partly to introduce the strange, sad episode that follows, in which Saul will seek the very prophet now lying in his grave at Ramah. Ellicott observes that the Septuagint, the Vulgate, and the Syriac dropped the clause, not understanding why it should be said twice.

The second hinge is Saul's own edict. The grammatical-historical Ellicott turns philologist over the terms: those that had familiar spirits renders ôboth, the plural of a word 'never explained with any certainty,' which scholars connect with a root meaning 'hollow,' hence 'one who speaks in a hollow voice' — the distended belly of the ventriloquist, which the Septuagint always has in view; while the wizards are literally 'the wise people,' a name given in irony to these dealers in forbidden arts. Clarke notes the barer fact that with Samuel dead there was now no accredited prophet to consult, and points the reader to the Mosaic law behind the edict (Lev. 19:31; Exod. 22:18).

The commentators are united on the sting of the arrangement. Saul's early zeal had put in force the clear command, Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live (Exod. 22:18; Lev. 20:27), which in the lax state of things had long lain in abeyance; and the verse quietly sets that zeal beside the errand on which the king is about to go, so that the reader sees, before Saul does, the depth of the self-contradiction into which despair is driving him. Ellicott adds that the ôb came in time to name not only the ventriloquist but the very spirit supposed to speak from the belly, so that the word covers both the practitioner and the demonic voice behind her (compare Isa. 29:4). There is no dispute here, only a shared sense that the historian has loaded a short verse with the whole irony of what follows — the dead prophet whom Saul will summon, and the outlawed art by which he will summon him, named side by side in a single breath.
4:
The narrative resumes with the drawing up of the two armies, and here the grammatical-historical commentators are entirely in their element, mapping the ground. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott together fix the positions: the Philistines advanced across the Valley of Jezreel and encamped at Shunem, on the south-western slope of the hill sometimes called Little Hermon, a little north of Jezreel; Saul mustered all Israel and pitched opposite them on Mount Gilboa, a range of white chalk hills rising some fifteen hundred feet, parted from the enemy only by the deep valley between. Keil notes that Shunem lay hardly two hours distant, so that from Gilboa the whole Philistine host could be plainly seen — a detail that will explain the terror of the next verse.

Ellicott enriches the topography with the memories that cling to Shunem. It was the home of Abishag the Shunammite (1 Kings 1:3), and the dwelling-place of the woman who entertained Elisha and whose dead son the prophet raised to life (2 Kings 4); modern travellers, he says, still find it a small hamlet with a spring, commanding a view as far as Carmel. Henry, for his part, marks that Shunem lay in the tribe of Issachar, a great way north of the Philistine country, which measures how deeply the enemy had penetrated. Gill contributes only the bare note that the Philistines had gathered from their several centres into a considerable army.

What the witnesses draw from the geography is the strategic shock of it. The Philistines are no longer contesting the familiar border 'marches' but have struck at the centre of the kingdom, challenging Israel on the great plain — a boldness that measures how far Saul's grip had loosened. Matthew Henry, in his comment on the opening verses, had already remarked that the land was ill-guarded, the king having worn out his strength pursuing David while leaving his people naked and exposed. The mountains face each other across the valley, the tents are in full view, and the stage is set for a battle Saul enters, as Henry says, with little heart, now that the Spirit of the LORD had departed from him.
5:
Saul sees the host and his heart greatly trembled, and the witnesses are unanimous that the true cause of his terror lay not in the enemy's numbers but in his own soul. The grammatical-historical commentators say so plainly. Keil and Delitzsch reason that since Saul had more than once been victorious over the Philistines, his sudden dread can hardly be explained except by the feeling that God had forsaken him, a feeling that now overwhelmed him. Ellicott grants that the enemy was more numerous than Saul had expected, but insists 'the real reason of his trembling must be looked for in the consciousness that God had forsaken him,' and paints the forsaken king standing on the white chalk of Gilboa, gazing at the long lines of Philistine tents on the opposite hill, wondering whether his old friend David was there among them in the division of Gath.

Clarke gathers the threefold ground of his fear into a sentence: Saul saw from the superiority of his enemies, from the state of his own army, and 'especially from his own state towards God,' that he had everything to fear. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley states the counter-truth in a breath: 'Had he kept close to God, he needed not fear all the armies of the Philistines.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry, commenting across these verses, presses the same lesson pastorally and turns the trembling into a study of conscience. Had Saul kept close to God he need not have feared at the sight of a Philistine army; but having provoked God to forsake him, his interest failed, his armies dwindled and looked mean, and, worse, his spirit failed him. Now he remembered the guilty blood of the Amalekites he had spared and the innocent blood of the priests he had spilt; his sins were set in order before his eyes, and a guilty conscience made him tremble at the shaking of a leaf. 'Troubles are terrors to the children of disobedience,' Henry concludes — and on that note the witnesses are wholly agreed: it was not the Philistines but a departed God that made the king afraid.
6:
Saul inquires of the LORD and receives no answer by any of the three appointed channels, and the witnesses divide their attention between the manner of the inquiry and the mystery of the silence. The reformed Gill fastens on the manner: the inquiry was not done truly or heartily, nor persevered in, so that it was as if he had not inquired at all — which is why Scripture can elsewhere say flatly that he inquired not of the LORD (1 Chron. 10:13-14). He asked, Gill and Henry both note, with a double, unstable mind, and with the secret design of consulting the devil if God would not answer.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the three media. Clarke lays them out — dreams, in which the suppliant begged a significant answer; Urim, the oracular response given through the high priest clothed with the ephod; and prophets, who would consult the LORD and report; adding that the only prophets then available were those of the schools Samuel had founded. Keil confirms that to inquire of the LORD is the standing phrase for seeking counsel through the Urim and Thummim, here widened to include all three means (compare Num. 27:21).

Ellicott raises the harder historical question the others pass over: how could Saul use the Urim at all, when since the massacre at Nob the priest who held the ephod, Abiathar, had fled to David's camp (1 Sam. 22:20)? He suggests that Saul, having desecrated Nob, set up a rival sanctuary at Gibeon under Zadok, who may have inquired for the king by an imitation ephod — but no response was received, and any prophets present, trained perhaps in Samuel's school, received no divine message either. The puritan-evangelical Henry supplies the theology of the silence: Saul had forfeited every method of inquiry, for how could one who had persecuted the prophets and slain the high priest expect to be answered by prophets or by Urim, or one who had sinned away the Spirit of grace be answered by dreams? 'Seek the LORD while he may be found, for there is a time when he will not be found.' The witnesses do not contend; together they hold that heaven's silence was not caprice but the just closing of doors Saul had himself barred.
7:
Rebuffed by heaven, Saul turns to hell, and the witnesses read his command — Seek me a woman that hath a familiar spirit — as the plunge that defines his ruin. The puritan-evangelical Henry frames it most vividly: finding no comfort from heaven or earth, Saul 'resolves to knock at the gates of hell.' Henry charges him with a double guilt — contempt of the God of Israel, as if a creature could help him when God had frowned, and flat contradiction of himself, for he now runs to that as an oracle which he had once condemned as an abomination and rooted out. 'It is common for men to inveigh severely against those sins which they are in no temptation to,' Henry observes, 'but afterwards to be themselves overcome by them.'

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the setting. Keil identifies the woman as a baalath-ob, the mistress of a conjuring spirit by which the dead were called up, and locates En-dor on the northern shoulder of Little Hermon (compare Josh. 17:11), only some ten English miles from the camp at Gilboa. Ellicott dwells on the pathos and the peril of the journey: this was 'the last spark' of Saul's old religious zeal turned into a wild and desperate superstition, so that the very class he had proscribed became his only resource — Flectere si nequeo superos, Acheronta movebo, 'if I cannot bend the powers above, I will stir up hell.' He notes the adventurous secrecy of the night march, which took the king round the eastern shoulder of the hill and past the very edge of the hostile camp, and records the Jewish tradition that the two companions were Abner and Amasa and that the witch was Abner's own mother, which would explain how she had escaped Saul's purge.

Clarke simply marks the strangeness — that a man who had banished the necromancers as dangerous impostors should now, when Jehovah had refused to help him, make one of them his only confidence. The witnesses do not debate; with one voice they see in the errand the logic of a heart forsaking God: shut out of the light, it will grope for any darkness that promises a word.
8:
Saul disguises himself, goes by night with two men, and asks the woman to divine by her familiar spirit and bring up whomever he shall name. The witnesses read the disguise first for its motive. The reformed Gill notes that Saul stripped off his royal robes and put on a peasant's or common soldier's dress, not only so the woman would not know him but so that he might slip from the camp unrecognised, none knowing that he was gone or whither. The wesleyan-arminian voices add the inner reason: Wesley says he disguised himself both because he was ashamed to be known guilty of such a practice and because he suspected the woman, had she known him, would not ply her art before him; Clarke agrees she might, being terrified, have refused.

The grammatical-historical commentators probe the request itself. Keil explains that bring me up defines the divining: prophecy by the ôb was performed by calling a departed spirit up from Sheol and obtaining from it disclosures concerning one's own fate. Ellicott, taking Keil's remark as his text, leaves it open whether the ôb was a spirit at the sorceress's service or the very soul of one already dead, compelled by some occult power to revisit the earth; and he pauses over the popular geography implied in bring me up — that Sheol, the abode of the departed, lay beneath the earth, as heaven lay above it. St. Paul speaks the same popular language of 'the lower parts of the earth' (Eph. 4:9), and the Christian creed has adopted it in saying that Christ descended into Hades. Ellicott presses the wider point that such 'black arts' have fascinated men in every age and every degree of civilisation, the dead being still invoked and questioned as they were in the half-barbarous night when Saul came to En-dor. Clarke adds the plain reason for the disguise — that the woman, terrified, would not have plied her art before the very king who had proscribed it — and Wesley the double motive, shame in Saul at being known guilty of such a practice, and fear that she would not otherwise perform. The witnesses agree that everything about the scene — the darkness, the disguise, the stealth — is the confession of a man who knows he is doing works of darkness and cannot bear the light.
9:
The woman's wary answer — thou knowest what Saul hath done — turns the stranger's own law against him, and the witnesses savour the irony while weighing her motive. The grammatical-historical Keil explains her difficulty: since Saul had driven the necromancers from the land, she feared the unknown visitor might be laying a snare for her soul, spying her out as a conjurer of the dead so as to inflict the capital penalty of the law (Lev. 20:27); and he stresses, from what follows, that at this point she plainly did not recognise Saul. Ellicott agrees that the Mosaic law, re-enacted by Saul in his early days, was clearly still in force, and that sorcerers had often been hunted down by informers, so the woman was on her guard — though he allows, with a touch of realism, that by enhancing the peril in which she stood she may have hoped to extort a larger present from the stranger who sought her aid.

The puritan-evangelical Henry seizes the providential sting of the moment: it was ordered that Saul should be told to his face of his own edict against witches at the very instant he was consulting one, 'for the greater aggravation of his sin.' And he draws a sharp portrait of the woman's conscience — sensible of danger from the edict of Saul, and full of care to guard against it, but 'not at all apprehensive of the obligations of God's law and the terrors of his wrath.' She considered what Saul had done, not what God had done, against such practices, and feared a snare laid for her life more than a snare laid for her soul. 'It is common,' Henry concludes, 'for sinners to be more afraid of punishment from men than of God's righteous judgment' — a verdict in which the whole company, mapping the same fear from their different angles, quietly concurs. Ellicott notes too that the old Jewish tradition, which made the woman's two visitors Abner and Amasa, would only heighten her caution, for she would be on her guard even before near kinsmen; and both he and Henry leave the impression of a woman shrewd about men and heedless of God, exactly answering the king who now stands before her in the dark.
10:
Saul swears by the LORD that no punishment shall befall her, and though the sources here are few, the witnesses find in the oath a window into the depth of his hardening. The reformed Gill is astonished at the sheer incongruity: 'it is much that Saul, being about such a work of darkness and wickedness, could take the name of the LORD into his mouth, and swear by him in such a solemn manner' — an act which must surely have reminded him of the very omniscience from which this wicked business could not be hid, and yet did not stay his hand. The Targum, Gill notes, softens it to swearing 'by the Word of the LORD,' but the scandal remains: the king invokes the God he is in the act of forsaking.

The grammatical-historical Keil reads the same oath as the measure of Saul's condition, calling it 'an oath which showed how utterly hardened Saul was.' To pledge the living God's name as security for a séance forbidden on pain of death is, in Keil's eyes, the sign of a conscience past feeling; and the very solemnity of the formula — As the LORD liveth, there shall no punishment happen to thee — only sets the profanity in sharper relief.

The witnesses do not differ, and the verse, brief as it is, gathers up the tragedy already unfolding. Saul had inquired of the LORD and been met with silence (v.6); now he swears by that same LORD to shield a crime the LORD had condemned, promising an impunity he had no power to grant, for, as Henry observes across these verses, he who could not secure himself could far less secure her from divine vengeance. It was Saul's plain duty as king to punish the woman, and he knew it, having framed the very law against her; yet he binds himself by oath not to do it, as though a man could by his own word release himself from a divine command. The oath is thus the pledge of a man using heaven's name to underwrite hell's work, and both the Reformed and the grammatical-historical witnesses read it as the last stage of a heart that has learned to speak of God while acting wholly against him.
11:
Reassured, the woman asks whom she shall bring up, and Saul names Samuel — and the witnesses read the request with a mixture of theological caution and deep human pity. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke is careful about what the woman thought she could do: by the professed rules of her art she meant no more than to have her 'familiar' personate whomever the client named, for over human souls in paradise or the pit such practitioners have no power. Fallen spirits, he adds, do not abound in knowledge; even Satan is far from excelling in wisdom, and men commonly credit him with far more than he possesses.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gathers the rabbinic lore and the pathos together. The Babylonian Talmud, he reports, debated whether the witch could really raise Samuel, and answered that it happened within twelve months of his death, while the soul was still thought to hover near the body. But Ellicott's chief interest is Saul's state of mind on the eve of Gilboa: feeling himself forsaken of God, the king's mind turns, in his utter desolation, to the friend and guide of his youth from whom he had been so hopelessly estranged; and there must have been a terrible struggle in that proud heart before he could stoop to seek help from one of the loathed and proscribed class. He quotes Archbishop Trench — 'there is something unutterably pathetic in the yearning of the dis-anointed king, now in his utter desolation, to exchange words once more with the friend and counsellor of his youth; and if he must hear his doom, to hear it from no other lips but his.'

Wesley says the same in little: Saul asked for Samuel 'whose kindness and compassion as he had formerly experienced, so now he expected it in his deep distress.' Gill and Keil simply record the exchange. There is no dispute yet — that will come the moment the apparition appears — but the witnesses are agreed on the poignancy: while Samuel lived at Ramah, near Gibeah, Saul never once went to consult him; now that he is dead, the cry is 'Bring me up Samuel,' and, as Henry notes elsewhere, many who despise God's ministers while living would be glad to have them again when they are gone.
12:
Here the chapter's great division opens, for the woman sees Samuel, cries out, and in the same instant knows her visitor is Saul — and the witnesses split over what, exactly, appeared. The reformed Gill takes her very terror as proof that this was not the true Samuel: no methods of raising him are described, he says, lest curiosity be tempted, and the whole tenor of the story tells against a genuine return of the prophet. But the wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the same cry the opposite way: 'that Samuel did appear on this occasion is most evident from the text,' and he was raised neither by the devil nor by the woman's incantations, for the appearance was something 'she was wholly unacquainted with' — her familiar did not come, and she was surprised and alarmed by an event utterly unlike her ordinary practice.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott stands with Clarke on the fact while canvassing the puzzles. Nothing is clearer, he says, than that the woman saw something she never dreamed of seeing; whether or not she was in general an impostor, on this occasion she was 'amazed and appalled at the success of her enchantments.' As to how she suddenly knew Saul, he weighs the guesses — Ewald's, that Samuel's shade rose with threatening gestures fit only for Saul; Keil's, that she fell into a clairvoyant state; and Josephus's, which Ellicott thinks the likeliest, that Samuel himself revealed the king's identity, as the blind Ahijah pierced the disguise of Jeroboam's wife (1 Kings 14:6). Wesley is briefest and boldest: 'the words are express, the woman saw Samuel,' God so ordering it for his own glory, and her loud cry of terror let her guess at once with whom she had been dealing.

So the witnesses part company. To Gill the apparition is a permitted deception; to Clarke, Ellicott, and Wesley it is Samuel himself, brought not by the witch's power but by God's overruling — the very unexpectedness of it, on their reading, being the surest sign that her art had nothing to do with it. Both sides, however, agree on one point that undercuts the necromancer's trade: whatever came up that night lay entirely beyond the woman's control, and her scream is the confession that she had raised a power she could not command.
13:
Saul, who has seen nothing himself, bids her not be afraid and asks what she saw, and she answers, I saw gods ascending out of the earth — a phrase over which the witnesses linger, chiefly the grammatical-historical school. Keil renders the plural elohim not as 'gods' nor 'God' but as 'a celestial being,' something super-terrestrial come up from the ground. Ellicott notes that the English Version follows most of the older translations in writing 'gods,' while the Chaldee gives 'an angel,' and that Cornelius à Lapide with the best modern scholars, reasoning from Saul's next question — What is his form? — takes the word to signify not a plurality but one God-like, majestic form. Yet Ellicott is honest that antiquity leaned the other way: a passage in the Talmud treatise Chaggigah speaks of two spirit-forms rising, Samuel and another, and one Jewish reading makes the second a robed 'judge' whose awful presence appalled the guilty woman.

The wesleyan-arminian voices concur that the appearance itself was extraordinary. Clarke observes that elohim is the very word used throughout Scripture for the Supreme Being, and that this sight — so unlike what she expected — alarmed the woman, for in it she could not recognise her familiar and was terrified at the appearance. Wesley smooths the grammar: she meant 'a god, a divine person, glorious and full of majesty,' exceeding not only mortal men but common ghosts, and used the plural either after the ordinary Hebrew idiom or after the heathen fashion of speaking of the dead.

Underneath the lexical question the witnesses agree on the effect, and the effect serves the larger argument. Whether one majestic form rose or two, the woman's fright is the same, and it tells, on the reading of Clarke, Ellicott, and Wesley, against her mastery of the event: she describes what she sees like an astonished spectator, not a practitioner in command of her craft. The rendering they debate — 'gods,' 'a god,' 'a celestial being,' 'an angel' — all point one way, to a visitant from beyond her summoning, and so the verse becomes another quiet witness that something more than ventriloquism was at work at En-dor.
14:
Pressed for the form, the woman answers, An old man cometh up, and he is covered with a mantle, and by these two marks Saul perceives it is Samuel and bows to the ground. The witnesses first weigh the recognition. Keil and Delitzsch and Wesley alike fasten on the mantle — the m'il which Samuel was accustomed to wear in life, the same robe Saul had once torn (1 Sam. 15:27) — as the token by which the king knew him. Keil adds a careful qualification: Saul himself does not appear to have seen the apparition, but that is no proof there was none, for 'it needs an opened eye, such as all do not possess, to see a departed spirit,' the bodily eyes not being enough.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the sequence as staged in two movements — first the 'gods' or angelic appearance that prepared the way, then the old man in the mantle — the whole so ordered as to show the woman that her incantations had not prevailed and that what now took place was independent of her art. Wesley draws the sharp logical point on which the identity turns: the mantle was the usual habit of prophets and particularly of Samuel; and 'if it was not Samuel, but another spirit in his shape, it is not true that Saul perceived it was Samuel' — a hint that the text's plain language favours a real appearance.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott meets the obvious objections head-on: how can a spirit bear the semblance of an old man, and how can such a being be clothed? He answers with Maimonides, who taught that such things — a white garment, a warrior's aspect — belong to 'an apparition of prophecy and a vision,' God clothing the invisible in forms our senses can grasp; and with Bishop Wordsworth, that God 'designed the spirit of Samuel should be recognised by human eyes,' and could do so only by objects visible to human sense, just as our Lord at the Transfiguration presented Moses in a recognisable form. So while Gill and Henry hold elsewhere that the shape was borrowed by a deceiving spirit, the majority here read the mantle and the reverent bow as marks of the true prophet, permitted by God to be known.
15:
Samuel's opening word — Why hast thou disquieted me, to bring me up? — and Saul's desperate complaint bring the chapter's central debate to its height. The puritan-evangelical Henry hears the whole exchange as a 'conference between Saul and Satan.' Citing Bishop Patrick, he argues that it is not in the power of witches to disturb the rest of good men, nor would the true Samuel have owned such a power in magic; the evil spirit personates the prophet to draw Saul's veneration and rivet him in the devil's interest. Saul's complaint Henry reads as the cry of a man enraged rather than penitent — he does not own the righteousness of God but flies out against him as unkind: 'God denies me, therefore I come to thee.'

The grammatical-historical commentators are more reserved about the identity. Keil notes that the word implies Samuel had indeed been disturbed from his rest in Hades (Isa. 14:9), but 'whether this had been effected by the conjuring arts of the witch, or by a miracle of God himself, is left undecided.' Ellicott sharpens the portrait of Saul: in the anguished cry I am sore distressed there is 'no confession of sin, no beseeching for mercy — nothing but the overmastering ambition to preserve himself.' He notes shrewdly that Saul, listing dreams and prophets, omits the Urim he had named before, and traces the omission to shame, for the Urim had been worn by Ahimelech, the high priest whom Saul had foully murdered (1 Sam. 22:18).

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke stands nearer the plain sense, taking the reproach to fall on Saul, not the woman, whose incantations 'had no influence in the business,' since the prophet appeared before she had well begun. Wesley mourns the timing: 'happy had it been if he had called Samuel sooner, or rather the God of Samuel! It was now too late.' So the verse leaves the witnesses genuinely divided — Henry and Gill certain it is a demon in the prophet's mantle, Keil holding the question open, Clarke and Wesley treating the speaker as Samuel himself — yet united on the pathos of a king who sought counsel everywhere but in repentance, and found only the sentence of his doom.
16:
The apparition throws Saul's question back upon him — Wherefore then dost thou ask of me, seeing the LORD is departed from thee, and is become thine enemy? — and the witnesses read the retort as the sealing of Saul's isolation. The reformed Gill, who holds the speaker to be the devil personating Samuel, still hears in the words a terrible truth reasoned out 'in such language as might be thought to be his own': how should Saul look for a friendly answer from the servant of a God who has become his foe? The grammatical-historical Keil ties the reproach to the old sentence of rejection, this being the reason first pronounced long before (1 Sam. 15:23): the LORD has turned enemy because Saul turned rebel.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke, reading the speaker as the true Samuel, hears the authentic accent of the prophet: 'Was ever I wont to give answers that were not dictated by the LORD? It is his counsel alone that I communicate.' The point matters for Clarke's larger case, for a lying spirit would hardly disclaim all speech but God's.

Ellicott turns to the text itself, noting that the rare Hebrew word rendered enemy occurs elsewhere only in Ps. 139:20, and looks like an Aramaic form; but since this book is otherwise written in pure classical Hebrew, he judges it more likely a copyist's slight alteration of the ordinary word for 'enemy,' while recording that the Septuagint and Vulgate read instead 'and is with thy neighbours.' The reading matters little to the sense, for either way the speaker declares that the LORD has taken David's side and left Saul's; the very help Saul sought is turned into the announcement that heaven is now arrayed against him. Beneath the textual nicety the witnesses converge: whether spoken by Samuel or by a spirit permitted to speak truth, the message strips Saul of his last illusion. He had come to the dead for the comfort the living God would not give, and he is told to his face that the God he has forsaken now stands against him — so that the séance yields not counsel but the confirmation of a doom already fixed.
17:
The voice announces that the LORD has done as he spake by me — rending the kingdom from Saul's hand and giving it to David — and this verse becomes the strongest single argument in the whole debate over who is speaking. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke seizes it: 'here was no illusion; none but Samuel could say this.' The words as he spake by me claim the very prophecy Samuel had once delivered in life, and to Clarke that authorship settles the identity. He adds a textual observation, that the opening the LORD hath done to him should almost certainly read to thee, a reading he finds in several of Kennicott's and De Rossi's manuscripts and in both the Venice Bibles of 1518, so that the sentence turns directly upon Saul.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the same conclusion from the moral side, citing Bishop Wordsworth: an evil spirit personating Samuel 'would not have spoken thus,' for he would not have wished to help David, the man after God's own heart, to the throne of Israel, nor would he have spoken in such solemn terms of the punishment due to rebellion against God. Ellicott also prefers the marginal rendering, that the LORD has 'done for himself,' accomplishing his own declared purpose.

The reformed Gill, holding to his view that the devil is representing Samuel, still concedes that the speaker here 'sometimes betrays himself' by speaking truth, and reads the reference to him as pointing to David, Saul's supposed enemy, now openly named as the heir. Thus the verse aligns the witnesses in a revealing way: those who argue for a real Samuel — Clarke and Ellicott with Wordsworth — rest their case chiefly on these words, contending that only the true prophet, or a spirit constrained by God, would advance David's cause and honour the divine justice; while Gill, unwilling to grant the necromancer a genuine prophet, must allow that even a deceiving spirit is here made to serve the truth. Either way, the sentence long ago spoken at Gilgal now returns to Saul on the eve of Gilboa, unrepealed and about to be fulfilled.
18:
The reason for the doom is now named: Because thou obeyedst not the voice of the LORD, nor executedst his fierce wrath upon Amalek. Though the sources here are brief, the witnesses are agreed that this word reaches back to the decisive turning-point of Saul's reign. The reformed Gill traces the sentence to Saul's disobedience in the matter of Amalek, when he spared what God had devoted to destruction; and, noting Abarbinel's view, he connects it also with the earlier failure at Gilgal — the two occasions on which the kingdom had first been threatened and then torn away (compare 1 Sam. 15:9). The wesleyan-arminian Clarke simply refers the reader back to that whole chapter for the story of the sin here charged (1 Sam. 15:1-9).

The verse's force, however, is drawn out most fully by the puritan-evangelical Henry, who reads it within the drama of the séance. It is a cruel stroke, he says, that the spirit should now fling in Saul's face the very sin it had once helped him to excuse. When Samuel in life had pressed that sin home to bring Saul to repentance, Satan had helped the king palliate it; now, to drive him to despair, the same tempter aggravates it. 'See what those get that hearken to Satan's temptations,' Henry warns: 'he himself will be their accuser, and insult over them' — the seducer to sin turned tormentor for it.

The witnesses do not dispute here; the verse is a single, backward-pointing indictment, and its weight lies in what it recalls. The disaster now closing over Saul at Gilboa is not a fresh misfortune but the ripening of an old disobedience: the command he had thought a small thing to soften, and the wrath he had thought a small thing to withhold, are shown at last to have been the hinge on which his whole reign turned. Henry marks the further cruelty that the words are spoken with an air of religion — the spirit ascribing all to God and even claiming to speak for him — so that Satan, who can transform himself into an angel of light, here quotes divine truth for a malicious end, to make Saul despair of the very mercy that the true Samuel would have set before him. What Saul had refused to execute upon Amalek, the LORD is about to execute upon Saul, and the long delay only proves how surely the word once spoken would stand.
19:
The sentence falls in full — Israel delivered to the Philistines, and to morrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me — and the witnesses agree on the terror of it while dividing gently over the last clause. The grammatical-historical Ellicott counts three crushing judgments here: the utter defeat of Israel's army, the violent death of Saul and his sons in the coming fight, and the sack of the camp; and he notes that the Hebrew machar need not mean the literal next day but the near future. Keil records the physical effect — Saul, till now kneeling, 'hastened and fell' his whole length upon the ground. The reformed Gill reads the speech as the work of a foul spirit bent on despair: not a word of comfort, no call to repentance or to recall David, only the announcement of ruin — much of which, he adds, might have been shrewdly guessed from the strength of the Philistines and the weakness of Israel.

The debate gathers on thou and thy sons shall be with me. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads it hopefully, quoting Charles Wesley's tender lines — 'Thou and thy sons, though slain, shall be / To-morrow in repose with me' — and holding that Saul had 'committed the sin unto death,' a sin visited with violent death while mercy was yet extended to the soul. Wesley cites the same verses, though granting the words 'may only mean, ye shall surely die.' Against this the grammatical-historical and latin-fathers witnesses read the phrase more soberly: Ellicott, with Bishop Wordsworth, takes it as a mild and charitable expression, 'thou shalt be as I am, no longer among the living,' by which the prophet draws Saul toward a better mind rather than pronouncing his final doom; and he quotes Augustine directly — 'shalt thou be with me does not refer to an equality in bliss, but to a like condition of death.' Henry too glosses it simply as 'in the state of the dead, separate from the body.' So the witnesses hold the terror in common — tomorrow the crown and the life are forfeit — while parting over its mercy: whether the dying king was given, in these words, a ray of hope, or only the plain announcement that he would soon be a shade like the prophet who spoke.
20:
Saul falls his full length upon the earth, undone by the words and by a whole day's fasting, and the witnesses read his collapse as body and conscience giving way together. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the physical account. Clarke notes that he fell 'with his own length,' swooning under the dreadful message; Ellicott adds that he had been listening in the reverent posture assumed in v.14, and now, crushed with terror and already weakened by the long fast and the rough night march from Gilboa, dropped prostrate. Keil marks the cause of the exhaustion — he had eaten no bread all that day and night, from sheer perturbation of mind.

The puritan-evangelical Henry makes the fall a mirror of the soul. Saul had come from the camp fasting, not for want of food but for want of appetite, his fear and his struggling conscience taking away all relish for it, so that he was 'an easy prey' to this fresh terror. He had asked to be told what he should do, and was told only what he had not done and what should be done to him. Henry drives the lesson home: 'those that expect any good counsel or comfort otherwise than from God, and in the way of his institutions, will be as wretchedly disappointed as Saul.' And he underlines the horror of the moment — though terrified even to despair, Saul 'was not humbled; he confessed not his sins, offered no sacrifices, and presented no supplications,' but in sullen despair rushed upon his doom.

Here a fine difference appears. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley is unwilling to close the door quite so firmly: all Saul's past sins, he supposes, were now brought to his remembrance, and 'what authority has any man to affirm that he felt no contrition all this time, although it did not seem good to the Holy Ghost to leave it upon record?' So while Henry sees only hardened despair and Gill and Clarke describe a man thunderstruck to the ground, Wesley leaves a narrow space for a grief the text does not report — a reminder, as the witnesses would have it, that God sets up such beacons to warn men not to stifle conviction, yet that while one repenting thought remains no sinner should count himself beyond mercy.
21:
The woman comes to the fallen king, sees his extremity, and pleads that she had first obeyed him at the hazard of her own life — and the witnesses read her approach for what it reveals about her, and against a theory of the story they reject. The grammatical-historical Keil corrects a common assumption: the phrase came unto Saul does not prove she had been shut in another room during the apparition, only that she had stood at some distance and now drew near to speak when he had fallen senseless. She had fulfilled his wish at the risk of her life, and now entreated him to grant hers — that she might set a morsel of bread before him and strengthen him for the road home.

Ellicott makes her conduct a decisive piece of evidence. The story, he says, is completed in these closing verses 'in a most natural and unaffected style,' and the witch, grievous sinner though she was, is here 'struck with a woman's pity for the stricken king,' doing her best with kind words and kinder acts to recover him from his death-like swoon. Her whole behaviour, he argues, contradicts the supposition that she had been moved by a bitter hatred of Saul to feign Samuel's appearance and imitate his voice by ventriloquism: first she was herself terrified at the apparition, and now she is saddened by the effect of the dead seer's words and labours to restore the king. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes only that she returned from the place to which she had withdrawn while Saul and Samuel conversed together.

The witnesses do not contend. Together they leave a strangely gentle picture at the story's end — a proscribed sorceress showing more tenderness to the ruined king than his own hardened heart shows to himself. Her pity, on Ellicott's reading, is one more quiet argument that the night's great appearance was no trick of hers: the woman who trembled at what rose from the earth, and who now grieves over the man it doomed, is plainly not the mistress of the scene but its frightened instrument.
22:
The woman presses her request — that as she had hearkened to Saul's voice, so he would now hearken to hers and take a little food for the journey — and the witnesses read the exchange as the first faint drawing of the crushed king back toward the world of the living. The reformed Gill sets out the reasonableness of her plea: she argues that since she had listened to him even at the peril of her own life, it was only fitting he should listen to her in a matter that made for his own preservation. The appeal is the plain logic of hospitality turned to the service of a broken man who no longer cares whether he lives.

The grammatical-historical Keil carries the scene forward. Saul at first refuses, but 'his servants, together with the woman, compelled him,' so that he yielded, rose from the ground where he had fallen, and sat upon the bench by the wall. Keil is careful to read the woman's motive charitably: she did all this, he says, 'from natural sympathy for the unhappy king,' and not, as some suppose, to remove suspicion of deception from Saul's mind — 'for she had not deceived the king at all.' The remark quietly aligns Keil with those who take the night's apparition to have been real and beyond her contriving.

The witnesses do not divide here; the verse is a small study in the ministry of ordinary kindness offered to a man under sentence of death. Saul had come to En-dor for a word from the grave and received his doom; now the only thing left to give him is a meal, and even that he must be pressed to take. Henry, surveying the scene, hears in the woman's plea a plain and reasonable exchange — she had hearkened to his voice at peril of her life, and asks that he now hearken to hers for the relief of his — and he finds a mournful fitness in a king reduced to so wretched a comforter. There is fitness too in the deeper irony — the king who would not be fed by the counsel of God at last consents to be fed, against his will, by the hand of a woman he had once outlawed, that he may have strength to walk back through the dark to the field where the word just spoken will be fulfilled.
23:
Saul refuses the food, is overborne by his servants and the woman, and at last rises and sits upon the bed — and the witnesses read both his refusal and his yielding. The reformed Gill weighs the refusal two ways: either Saul, in his despair, chose to die of famine rather than by the Philistine sword, or, more simply, he could not eat, his stomach loathing all food because he was overwhelmed with grief and trouble. Either reading shows a man for whom the appetite for life itself had failed.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the yielding and the setting. Keil describes how, once pressed, Saul rose from the earth and sat upon the mittah, the cushioned bench along the wall furnished with pillows. Clarke and Ellicott clear away a popular error about the scene: the bed was simply the divan or couch on which the ancients reclined to take their meals, and there is nothing in the narrative to support the familiar picture, so often painted, of the whole event taking place in a cave — the witch, they judge, lived in an ordinary dwelling of her own at En-dor, not a place of concealment.

The puritan-evangelical Henry draws the lesson from the manner of the compelling. His servants and the woman prevailed not by force but by friendly and rational persuasion, and Henry takes this as the true sense of the parable's word compel them to come in (Luke 14:23): a courteous constraint, not a violent one. 'How forcible are right words,' he adds with Job (Job 6:25), 'when men are pressed by them to that which is for their own interest.' Yet Henry does not pretend the food could reach Saul's real trouble: 'What are dainty meats to a wounded conscience? As vinegar upon nitre, so is he that sings songs to a heavy heart.' Clarke, for his part, is not surprised that Saul's appetite had failed with his strength, so heavy was the message that had felled him. The witnesses agree in the picture — a man raised from the floor and coaxed to the couch, his body persuaded to what his soul had refused, the outward kindness unable to touch the inward despair.
24:
The woman hastens to prepare a meal — killing the fat calf she kept in the house and baking unleavened bread — and the witnesses read her speed and her generosity together. The reformed Gill notes that a fat calf was reckoned a choice dish in those countries, fit for any guest, and reports that Josephus highly commends the woman's liberality as worthy of imitation, she having fed and cared for the animal in her own house and now freely spending it on a stranger. The grammatical-historical Keil reads the whole act, as before, as flowing from her 'natural sympathy for the unhappy king' rather than from any calculation.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke explains the practical details from Eastern custom. The ancients, he says, used great dispatch in their cookery, for in hot countries flesh could not be kept, and so they commonly had young animals — calves, lambs, kids — ready for immediate slaughter; when a guest came, one was killed and dressed at once. The bread was unleavened, both Clarke and Wesley observe, simply because there was no time to let the leaven work through the dough; haste, not ritual, dictated it.

The witnesses find no ground for dispute in so plain a verse, but they linger over the pathos of the contrast. Here is open-handed, immediate hospitality — the best the house can offer, prepared in haste and pressed upon the guest — spread before a king who has just heard that he and his sons will be dead before another such meal is eaten. Gill notes that the word for the calf suggests one used in treading out the corn, a working animal the woman could ill spare, which only heightens her generosity; and Josephus, he reports, held her up as a pattern of readiness to relieve the distressed even where there is no prospect of recompense. The very abundance of the table sharpens the tragedy: the woman gives freely of the life of her herd to a man from whom life itself is being taken, and the warm ordinariness of killing a calf and baking bread stands in mute contrast to the sentence of doom that hangs, unspoken now, over the little supper at En-dor.
25:
They eat, rise, and depart the same night, and on this closing verse the witnesses draw together both the interpretation of the whole episode and their verdict on Saul. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke uses the finality of the departure to sum up his reading of the chapter. All its events, he notes, fill a single night: Saul came, consulted the woman, heard Samuel, was overwhelmed, was fed, and went — and the battle followed, in all likelihood, the very next day. He then sets down his settled conviction in a series of articles: that there is a real world of conscious spirits; that no incantation has power over a departed saint of God; that the woman had no power over Samuel; and yet that 'Samuel did actually appear to Saul,' sent by the especial mercy of God to warn the doomed king, so that the oracle of the coming defeat and death — which neither human nor diabolical wisdom could foresee — came from God himself and no evil spirit.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott, quoting von Gerlach, reads the departure the other way, as the measure of Saul's ruin: 'Saul was too hardened in his sin to express any grief or pain, either on his own account or because of the fate of his sons and his people. In stolid desperation he went to meet his fate.' This, von Gerlach adds, was 'the terrible end of a man whom the Spirit of God had once taken possession of and turned into another man,' and endowed with gifts to lead the people of God. Keil closes on the same note of a hardened heart walking knowingly to its doom.

The puritan-evangelical Henry, surveying the scene, turns it into a beacon. Saul rose and went before it was light, both to hasten to his post and to steal unseen from so scandalous a house; and Henry marvels less at his courage in returning to a battle he knew was lost than at 'the hardness of his heart, that he did not again apply to God by repentance and prayer.' So the chapter ends where the witnesses have stood throughout — divided over the metaphysics of what rose from the earth, whether the true Samuel by God's mercy or a spirit permitted to speak, but wholly agreed on the tragedy of the man who sought it: a king who, shut out from the light he had forsaken, groped in the dark for a word, and found only the confirmation of his end.
